content.problemStatement:
You are given a prime ppp and a matrix MMM with NNN rows (numbered 111 through NNN) and NNN columns (numbered 111 through NNN). For each row rrr and column ccc, the cell in row rrr and column ccc can either be empty or contain an integer Mr,cM_{r, c}Mr,c​. Initially, all cells are empty.

Now you should process QQQ queries. In each query, you are given integers xxx, yyy and vvv and you should do the following:


If the cell (x,y)(x,y)(x,y) is empty before this query, put the value vvv in it, i.e. set Mx,yM_{x, y}Mx,y​ to vvv.
Otherwise, i.e. if the cell (x,y)(x,y)(x,y) is not empty, make this cell empty again. It is guaranteed that in such a case, Mx,y=vM_{x, y} = vMx,y​=v before this query; vvv is provided for convenience.
Afterwards, Chef wants you to find the number of ways to fill all empty cells with (not necessarily the same) integers in such a way that the resulting matrix is curious. Since this number may be large, compute it modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
In particular, when there are no empty cells in the matrix, the answer is 111 if the matrix is curious or 000 if it is not curious.

In a curious matrix:


Each cell contains an integer between 111 and p−1p-1p−1 inclusive.
For each non-trivial square submatrix AAA of MMM (a submatrix containing more than one cell), its determinant ∣A∣|A|∣A∣ is a multiple of ppp.
For example, consider the following matrix.

A=[121242242]A=
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 2 & 1 \\
2 & 4 & 2 \\
2 & 4 & 2 \\
\end{bmatrix}A=​122​244​122​​This matrix is curious. For the prime p=5p=5p=5, each of the elements of the matrix is in the range [1,p−1][1,p-1][1,p−1]. Also, the determinant of each non-trivial square submatrix (there are five of them) is a multiple of the given prime ― for example, the determinant of the whole matrix is 000.

B=[3114]B=
\begin{bmatrix}
3 & 1 \\
1 & 4 \\
\end{bmatrix}B=[31​14​]The above matrix is not a curious matrix for p=5p=5p=5, since the determinant of the only non-trivial square submatrix (which is the whole matrix) is 111111, not a multiple of ppp.
constraints: **Subtask #2 (20 points):** no two queries affect the same cell, i.e. the pairs (x,y)(x,y)(x,y) for all queries are pairwise distinct
**Subtask #3 (20 points):** after each query, there is at least one way to construct a curious matrix
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains three space-separated integers NNN, QQQ and ppp.
QQQ lines follow. Each of these lines contains three space-separated integers xxx, yyy and vvv describing a query.
content.outputFormat: After performing each query, print a single line containing one integer ― the number of ways to form a curious matrix, modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
The only non-trivial square submatrix here is the whole matrix.
In the 444-th query, the matrix is completely filled, but it is not curious for p=5p=5p=5, so the answer is 000.
After the 555-th query, there are 333 filled cells: (1,1)→3(1,1) \rightarrow 3(1,1)→3, (2,1)→1(2,1) \rightarrow 1(2,1)→1 and (2,2)→4(2,2) \rightarrow 4(2,2)→4. The cell (1,2)(1,2)(1,2) is empty and if we want the determinant of the matrix to be divisible by p=5p=5p=5, we have to put 222 in this cell. Then the determinant is 3⋅4−1⋅2=103 \cdot 4 - 1 \cdot 2 = 103⋅4−1⋅2=10.
